title: Database Management
description: Introduction to databases, DBMS, RDBMS, and normalization concepts.

sections.title: Data, Information & Metadata
sections.lesson:
Data, Information, and Metadata

In computing and information systems, the terms data, information, and metadata are closely related but not the same. Understanding their differences is important when working with databases, information systems, and data analysis.

1. What is Data?

Data refers to raw, unprocessed facts and figures. By themselves, data may not have meaning until they are organized or interpreted.

Examples of data:

  • Numbers: 85, 90, 72
  • Text: "Juan", "Maria"
  • Dates: 2025-01-15
  • Sensor readings: 36.5°C
Key idea: Data is raw and does not explain itself.

2. What is Information?

Information is data that has been processed, organized, or summarized so that it becomes meaningful and useful.

Examples of information:

  • "The average score of the class is 82."
  • "Maria scored the highest grade in the exam."
  • "The temperature today is higher than yesterday."
Key idea: Information answers questions like what happened? or what does it mean?

3. What is Metadata?

Metadata is often described as data about data. It provides additional details that describe, explain, or give context to data.

Examples of metadata:

  • Date a file was created or modified
  • File size and file type (e.g., PDF, JPG)
  • Author of a document
  • Column names and data types in a database table
Key idea: Metadata helps us understand, manage, and organize data.

4. Simple Comparison

Term Description Example
Data Raw facts and figures 85, 90, 72
Information Processed and meaningful data Average score is 82
Metadata Data about data Exam date, student ID

5. Activity: Data, Information, or Metadata?

Identify whether each item below is Data, Information, or Metadata. Write your answer beside each number.

  1. Student name: "Carlos Dela Cruz"
  2. "The total number of students who passed is 45."
  3. Date when the report was created
  4. Scores: 78, 85, 91, 88
  5. File size of a document: 2.3 MB
  6. "The highest temperature recorded today is 34°C."
  7. Column name in a database table: birth_date
Tip:
  • If it is raw → Data
  • If it explains or summarizes → Information
  • If it describes other data → Metadata

sections.title: Traditional File System vs DBMS
sections.lesson:
Traditional File Processing vs. Database Approach

Before modern database systems existed, organizations stored and managed data using traditional file processing systems. As data volume and complexity increased, the database approach was introduced to overcome the limitations of file-based systems.

1. Traditional File Processing System

In a traditional file processing system, each application program manages its own data files. The data is stored in separate files, usually designed specifically for a particular application.

Characteristics:

  • Data is stored in flat files (text files, spreadsheets, etc.)
  • Each application has its own files
  • Programs and data are tightly coupled
  • Data access logic is written directly into the program
Common Problems:
  • Data redundancy (duplicate data)
  • Data inconsistency
  • Difficult data sharing
  • High maintenance cost

2. Database Approach

The database approach uses a centralized database managed by a Database Management System (DBMS). Multiple applications can access the same data without duplication.

Characteristics:

  • Data is stored in a centralized database
  • Multiple applications share the same data
  • Data independence between programs and data
  • DBMS controls access, security, and integrity
Advantages:
  • Reduced data redundancy
  • Improved data consistency
  • Better data security
  • Easier data sharing and reporting

3. Simple Illustration

Traditional File Processing:


Payroll Program  → payroll.txt
Enrollment App   → students.txt
Library System   → members.txt

Database Approach:


Payroll Program
Enrollment App   → Central Database (DBMS)
Library System

4. Comparison Table

Aspect File Processing Database Approach
Data Storage Separate files Centralized database
Redundancy High Low
Data Sharing Difficult Easy
Security Limited Controlled by DBMS
Maintenance Hard to maintain Easier to maintain

5. Activity: File Processing or Database Approach?

Identify whether each situation describes a Traditional File Processing System or a Database Approach.

  1. Each department keeps its own Excel file of employee records.
  2. A school system uses one centralized database accessed by enrollment, grading, and billing modules.
  3. The same student data appears in multiple files across different programs.
  4. User access and permissions are controlled by a DBMS.
  5. Updating a data format requires modifying several programs.
  6. Multiple applications retrieve data using SQL queries.
Guide:
  • Separate files per program → File Processing
  • Centralized data with DBMS → Database Approach

sections.title: Relational Database Management System
sections.lesson:
What is a Database?

A database is an organized collection of structured data stored electronically in a computer system. It is designed to allow efficient storage, retrieval, and management of large volumes of data.

Databases are essential in modern applications such as enrollment systems, payroll systems, social media platforms, and online shopping applications.

Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)

A Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) is a type of DBMS that stores data in a structured format using tables, also known as relations, entities, data collections, and database objects.

RDBMS ensures data integrity, supports SQL (Structured Query Language), and allows relationships to be created between tables.

Key Features of RDBMS
  • Data is stored in tables (relations)
  • Supports ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)
  • Provides data integrity using primary and foreign keys
  • Allows complex data queries using SQL

Relational Model Concepts

The relational model is the foundation of RDBMS. It represents data in the form of tables and defines how data is stored, accessed, and manipulated.

Tables

A table is a collection of related data organized into rows and columns.

  • Rows (Tuples, Records) – represent individual records
  • Columns (Attributes, Fields) – represent specific properties of data
Example: Employee Table 
EmployeeID | Name       | Department | Salary
--------------------------------------------
101        | John Doe   | HR         | 50000
102        | Jane Smith | IT         | 60000

Keys in a Database

Keys are used to uniquely identify records and to establish relationships between tables.

Primary Key

A primary key uniquely identifies each record in a table and cannot contain null values.

Example: EmployeeID in the Employee table.

Foreign Key

A foreign key is a field in one table that refers to the primary key of another table. It is used to create relationships between tables.

Table Relationships

Relationships define how tables are connected with each other in a database.

  • One-to-One (1:1) – one record relates to one record
  • One-to-Many (1:N) – one record relates to multiple records
  • Many-to-Many (M:N) – multiple records relate to multiple records

Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams

ER Diagrams visually represent the database structure, including entities, attributes, and relationships.

  • Entities – real-world objects (e.g., Employee, Department)
  • Attributes – properties of entities (e.g., EmployeeID, Name)
  • Relationships – connections between entities
Common ER Diagram Symbols
  • Rectangle – Entity
  • Ellipse – Attribute
  • Diamond – Relationship
  • Lines – Connections

Key Takeaway

The relational model and proper database design principles are fundamental to creating efficient, reliable, and scalable database systems.

sections.title: Database Normalization
sections.lesson:
Module: Database Design Fundamentals

Relational database normalization is a systematic approach to organizing data in a database to minimize redundancy and improve data integrity. The goal is to store data once, in the correct place, while preserving logical relationships among tables.

In simple terms: Normalization means structuring your tables so that each fact is stored only once.

Why Normalization Is Important

Poorly designed databases often suffer from duplicated data, inconsistent values, and unexpected data loss. Normalization helps eliminate these problems by organizing tables based on functional dependencies.

  • Reduces data redundancy
  • Prevents update, insertion, and deletion anomalies
  • Improves data integrity and consistency
  • Supports scalability and easier maintenance
Key idea: Correct structure first, performance tuning later.

Data Redundancy and Anomalies

Data redundancy occurs when the same information is stored in multiple rows or tables. This often leads to three common anomalies:

  • Update anomaly: Changing data in one row but forgetting others
  • Insertion anomaly: Inability to add data without unrelated information
  • Deletion anomaly: Loss of important data when removing records
Example: Deleting the last student enrolled in a course may also remove the course details.

First Normal Form (1NF)

A table is in First Normal Form (1NF) if:

  • Each field contains atomic (indivisible) values
  • No repeating groups or multi-valued attributes exist
  • Each record can be uniquely identified
-- Not in 1NF
StudentID | Courses
101       | IT101, IT102

-- In 1NF
StudentID | CourseID
101       | IT101
101       | IT102
Key idea: One cell, one value.

Second Normal Form (2NF)

A table is in Second Normal Form (2NF) if:

  • It is already in 1NF
  • All non-key attributes depend on the entire primary key

This rule applies mainly to tables with composite primary keys.

-- Partial dependency problem
(StudentID, CourseID) → StudentName

-- Decomposed tables
Students(StudentID, StudentName)
Enrollments(StudentID, CourseID)
Key idea: No partial dependency on a composite key.

Third Normal Form (3NF)

A table is in Third Normal Form (3NF) if:

  • It is already in 2NF
  • No transitive dependencies exist

A transitive dependency occurs when a non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute rather than the primary key. 

-- Transitive dependency
CourseID → Instructor → InstructorPhone

-- Decomposed tables
Courses(CourseID, InstructorID)
Instructors(InstructorID, InstructorPhone)
Key idea: Non-key attributes should depend only on the primary key.

Summary of Normal Forms

Normal Form Main Purpose
1NF Removes repeating and multi-valued fields
2NF Eliminates partial dependency
3NF Eliminates transitive dependency

Normalization in Practice

Most transactional systems (OLTP) use databases normalized up to Third Normal Form to ensure data accuracy and consistency. In reporting or analytical systems (OLAP), controlled denormalization may be used to improve performance.

Design rule: Normalize for correctness, denormalize only when performance requires it.

Final Takeaway

Normalization is a foundational concept in relational database design. A well-normalized database leads to cleaner data, fewer errors, and more reliable applications.

Remember: Good structure today prevents problems tomorrow.

sections.title: Introduction to SQL
sections.lesson:
Introduction to SQL

SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language used to communicate with relational databases. It allows users to create databases, define tables, store data, retrieve information, and manage database security.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand what SQL is and why it is important
  • Identify common SQL concepts and terminology
  • Recognize different SQL environments and tools
  • Set up a basic SQL working environment
  • Create a database and tables using SQL
  • Identify appropriate data types for table columns

What is SQL?

SQL is a language designed for managing data stored in a relational database. Instead of navigating files manually, SQL allows users to issue commands that tell the database what to do.

Common tasks performed using SQL include:

  • Creating databases and tables
  • Inserting, updating, and deleting records
  • Retrieving data using queries
  • Controlling access and permissions
Key idea: SQL focuses on what data you want, not how the database internally finds it.

Basic SQL Concepts

  • Database – A collection of organized data
  • Table – A structure that stores data in rows and columns
  • Row (Record) – A single entry in a table
  • Column (Field) – A specific attribute of the data
  • Primary Key – A unique identifier for each record

SQL Environment

An SQL environment consists of a Database Management System (DBMS) and a tool or interface used to write and execute SQL commands.

Common DBMS examples:

  • MySQL
  • MariaDB
  • PostgreSQL
  • SQLite
  • Microsoft SQL Server

SQL Interface

SQL commands can be executed using different interfaces such as:

  • Command-line tools
  • Graphical tools (e.g., phpMyAdmin, MySQL Workbench)
  • Web-based database management tools
Note: Regardless of the interface, the SQL syntax remains mostly the same.

Setting Up the SQL Environment

To start using SQL, the following are typically required:

  1. Install a DBMS (e.g., MySQL or MariaDB)
  2. Install a database interface or management tool
  3. Start the database service
  4. Log in using valid credentials

Creating a Database

The following SQL statement creates a new database:

CREATE DATABASE school_db;

To select and use the database:

USE school_db;

Creating a Table

Tables store actual data inside a database. Each column must be defined with a name and data type. 

CREATE TABLE students (
    student_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    full_name VARCHAR(100),
    age INT,
    birth_date DATE
);

Identifying SQL Data Types

Choosing the correct data type is important for data accuracy and efficiency.

  • INT – Whole numbers
  • VARCHAR(n) – Text with variable length
  • DATE – Date values
  • DECIMAL(p,s) – Exact numeric values with decimals
  • BOOLEAN – True or false values
Tip: Always select the smallest data type that can correctly store your data.

Adding Constraints (Primary Key and Foreign Key)

Constraints are rules applied to table columns to enforce data integrity and accuracy. Two of the most important constraints in relational databases are Primary Key and Foreign Key.

Primary Key

A Primary Key uniquely identifies each record in a table. It ensures that the value is unique and not null.

  • Each table should have only one primary key
  • Often applied to ID columns
student_id INT PRIMARY KEY
Foreign Key

A Foreign Key is used to link two tables together. It references the primary key of another table, ensuring that relationships between tables remain valid.

  • Prevents invalid data from being inserted
  • Maintains relationships between tables
FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id)
Key idea: Primary keys identify records, while foreign keys connect related tables.

Summary

SQL is a powerful and essential tool for managing relational databases. By understanding its environment, syntax, and core concepts, users can efficiently design databases and interact with stored data.
